content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

Note: Before executing script examples check if they are in global .claude or local .claude.

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format

1→# AI Daemon
     2→
     3→A lightweight AI framework built on Spring Boot 4 and Spring AI. Connect any LLM provider, extend capabilities with skills and MCPs, schedule autonomous jobs, and interact through REST, Web UI, or terminal -- all from a single self-hosted service.
     4→
     5→![Example use case](images/example.png)
     6→
     7→## Architecture
     8→
     9→```mermaid
    10→graph TB
    11→    User([User])
    12→
    13→    subgraph Interface["Interface Layer"]
    14→        REST[REST API]
    15→        WebUI[Web UI]
    16→        CLI[Interactive CLI]
    17→    end
    18→
    19→    User -->|HTTP / curl| REST
    20→    User -->|Browser| WebUI
    21→    User -->|Terminal| CLI
    22→    WebUI --> REST
    23→
    24→    subgraph Engine["AI Engine"]
    25→        Chat[Chat Service]
    26→        Conv[Conversation Manager]
    27→        Providers[Provider Registry]
    28→        Memory[Persistent Memory]
    29→        Skills[Skills Reader]

    84→### Delegation
    85→
    86→When delegation is enabled, the main agent can spawn sub-agents to handle subtasks in parallel. Sub-agents run asynchronously; when they finish, the parent is woken with a status update and can synthesize results or send revision work.
    87→Sub-agents can create their own subagents. The diagram shows one parent and only top subagents for simplicitly.
    88→
    89→```mermaid
    90→sequenceDiagram
    91→    participant User
    92→    participant Parent as Parent Agent
    93→    participant DelegationService
    94→    participant Sub1 as Sub-agent 1
    95→    participant Sub2 as Sub-agent 2
    96→
    97→    User->>Parent: Message
    98→    Parent->>Parent: Estimates time > threshold
    99→    Parent->>DelegationService: delegateToSubAgent x2
   100→    DelegationService->>Sub1: Create and run sub-conversation
   101→    DelegationService->>Sub2: Create and run sub-conversation
   102→    Parent->>User: Delegated, will respond when ready
   103→    Sub1->>DelegationService: Completed
   104→    Sub2->>DelegationService: Completed
   105→    DelegationService->>Parent: Delegation Status Update with sub results
   106→    Parent->>Parent: Review, addWorkToSubAgent or synthesize
   107→    Parent->>User: Final response
   108→```
   109→
   110→- **Tools:** `delegateToSubAgent(name, instruction)` creates a sub-conversation and queues it. `addWorkToSubAgent(subConversationId, instruction)` sends follow-up work to a sub-agent after reviewing its output.
   111→- **Config:** `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`, `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds=30`. The model is instructed to delegate when its estimated time exceeds the threshold.
   112→
   113→## Features
   114→
   115→- **Multi-provider** -- OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Ollama. Add/remove providers at runtime with API key persistence.
   116→- **Conversations** -- Stateful multi-turn sessions with full chat history, persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. The provider (agent) is selected **per prompt**: set or change it for the conversation so the next message uses that provider. Conversations have creation timestamps and can be sorted by time.
   117→- **Streaming** -- Conversation messages can be sent with `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` (SSE). Chunks include `reasoning`, `answer`, and `tool` types so the UI can show thinking and tools in real time.
   118→- **Delegation** -- Optional sub-agent delegation: the AI splits work into sub-conversations that run in parallel; when they complete, the parent is notified and can synthesize or request revisions. Enable with `aidaemon.delegation-enabled=true`.
   119→- **Thinking / reasoning** -- Supported for providers that expose it (e.g. Anthropic extended thinking). Reasoning is streamed separately and shown in the Web UI; scheduled job results include thinking in the stored output.
   120→- **Prompt caching** -- Conversation-history and prompt caching are used where supported (e.g. Anthropic, Gemini) to reduce cost and latency.
   121→- **Context window** -- Optional `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` (character limit) trims older messages and, for named bots, personal memory so the prompt fits. Use `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` (0–1) to split the budget between conversation history and bot personal memory. The AI can use the `retrieveOlderMessages` tool to fetch older conversation content when context is trimmed.
   122→- **Persistent memory** -- AI can save and recall information across sessions via `memory.json`.
   123→- **Skills** -- Drop instruction files into `~/.aidaemon/skills/` or install from [Smithery](https://smithery.ai) via REST endpoint. The AI reads them for domain-specific context.
   124→- **MCP support** -- Connect remote (Streamable HTTP, SSE) and local (stdio) MCP servers. Drop JSON configs into `~/.aidaemon/mcps/` and reload, or add [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCPs via chat (Notion, Google Calendar, etc.).
   125→- **Scheduled jobs** -- AI creates cron jobs (recurring or one-time) that autonomously execute instructions on schedule. AI can also list and cancel them.
   126→- **Shell access** -- Optionally allow the AI to execute host commands (git, docker, curl, etc.). Controlled by a global toggle endpoint.
   127→- **Web UI** -- React app in `frontend/webui/` for providers, conversations, and streaming chat with reasoning and tool visibility.
   128→- **Interactive CLI** -- Terminal-based chat with provider selection, conversation management, and shell toggle.
   129→
   130→## Prerequisites
   131→
   132→- Java 25+
   133→- Maven (or use the included `mvnw` wrapper)
   134→
   135→## Quick Start
   136→
   137→```bash
   138→# Clone and build
   139→git clone <repo-url>
   140→cd aidaemon
   141→./mvnw spring-boot:run
   142→```
   143→
   144→On Windows PowerShell:
   145→```powershell
   146→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run
   147→```
   148→
   149→The server starts on `http://localhost:8080`.
   150→
   151→### 1. Register a Provider
   152→
   153→```bash
   154→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   155→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   156→  -d '{"name":"my-gpt","type":"OPENAI","apiKey":"...","model":"gpt-4o"}'
   157→```
   158→
   159→Supported types: `OPENAI`, `ANTHROPIC`, `GEMINI`, `OLLAMA`
   160→
   161→For Ollama (no API key needed):
   162→```bash
   163→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/providers \
   164→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   165→  -d '{"name":"local-llama","type":"OLLAMA","baseUrl":"http://localhost:11434","model":"llama3"}'
   166→```
   167→
   168→### 2. Chat (Stateless)
   169→
   170→```bash
   171→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/chat/{providerId} \
   172→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   173→  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'
   174→```
   175→
   176→### 3. Conversations (Stateful)
   177→
   178→Conversations are persisted under `~/.aidaemon/conversations/`. Each has an optional name. The provider is selected per prompt: set it when creating or via `PATCH`; the next message you send will use that provider.
   179→
   180→```bash
   181→# Create a conversation
   182→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations \
   183→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   184→  -d '{"name":"My chat","providerId":"<id>"}'
   185→
   186→# Send messages (history is maintained server-side)
   187→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages \
   188→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   189→  -d '{"message":"What did I just ask you?"}'
   190→
   191→# Stream response (SSE: reasoning, answer, tool chunks)
   192→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream \
   193→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   194→  -d '{"message":"Explain X"}' \
   195→  -N
   196→
   197→# Set which provider to use for the next prompt(s)
   198→curl -X PATCH http://localhost:8080/api/conversations/{id} \
   199→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   200→  -d '{"providerId":"other-provider-id"}'
   201→```
   202→
   203→### 4. Interactive CLI
   204→
   205→```bash
   206→./mvnw spring-boot:run -Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli
   207→```
   208→
   209→On Windows PowerShell:
   210→```powershell
   211→.\mvnw.cmd spring-boot:run "-Dspring-boot.run.profiles=cli"
   212→```
   213→
   214→Commands inside CLI: `/quit`, `/new` (new conversation), `/shell` (toggle shell access).
   215→
   216→### 5. Web UI
   217→
   218→From the project root, build and run the React UI (proxy to the backend):
   219→
   220→```bash
   221→cd frontend/webui
   222→npm install
   223→npm run dev
   224→```
   225→
   226→Connect to the URL shown (e.g. Vite dev server). Configure providers and conversations, then chat; responses stream with reasoning and tool calls when available.
   227→
   228→## Skills
   229→
   230→Skills are folders containing a `SKILL.md` and optional resources (scripts, references, assets) that give the AI domain-specific knowledge.
   231→
   232→**Manual install:** Drop a skill folder into `~/.aidaemon/skills/`. With Smithery MCP enabled, ask the AI to search and install skills (e.g. "install the skill-creator skill from Smithery"); it uses `searchSmitherySkills` and `installSmitherySkill`.
   233→
   234→**Manage skills:** Ask the AI to list skills (`listSkills`), list or read files in a skill (`listSkillFiles`, `readSkillFile`), or remove a skill (`removeSkill`).
   235→
   236→## MCP Servers
   237→
   238→Create JSON config files in `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`.
   239→
   240→**Remote (Streamable HTTP):**
   241→```json
   242→{
   243→  "name": "github",
   244→  "type": "REMOTE",
   245→  "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
   246→  "headers": {
   247→    "Authorization": "Bearer <token>"
   248→  }
   249→}
   250→```
   251→
   252→**Remote (Legacy SSE):**
   253→```json
   254→{
   255→  "name": "my-sse-server",
   256→  "type": "SSE",
   257→  "url": "https://example.com/mcp/sse"
   258→}
   259→```
   260→
   261→**Local (stdio):**
   262→```json
   263→{
   264→  "name": "aviation",
   265→  "type": "LOCAL",
   266→  "command": "npx",
   267→  "args": ["-y", "aviationstack-mcp-server"],
   268→  "env": {
   269→    "AVIATIONSTACK_API_KEY": "<key>"
   270→  }
   271→}
   272→```
   273→
   274→### Adding MCP via Smithery
   275→
   276→You can connect [Smithery](https://smithery.ai)-hosted MCP servers (e.g. Notion, Google Calendar) without writing config files by hand.
   277→
   278→1. **Enable and configure Smithery MCP** in `application.yaml` or environment:
   279→
   280→   ```yaml
   281→   aidaemon:
   282→     smithery-mcp:
   283→       enabled: true
   284→       namespace: <your-smithery-namespace>
   285→       api-key: <your-smithery-api-key>
   286→   ```
   287→
   288→   Get your namespace and API key from the [Smithery dashboard](https://smithery.ai).
   289→
   290→2. **Find a server** on [smithery.ai/servers](https://smithery.ai/servers) and note its slug (e.g. `notion`, `googlecalendar`) or full URL (e.g. `https://smithery.ai/servers/notion`).
   291→
   292→3. **Add the MCP via chat:** In any conversation, ask the AI to add the server by slug or URL, for example:
   293→   - “Add the Notion MCP from Smithery”
   294→   - “Add Smithery MCP server googlecalendar”
   295→
   296→   The AI uses the `addSmitheryMcp` tool, writes the config to `~/.aidaemon/mcps/`, and connects the server.
   297→
   298→4. **OAuth:** If the server requires sign-in, the tool returns an authorization URL. Open it in your browser, complete sign-in, then reload MCPs:
   299→
   300→   ```bash
   301→   curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   302→   ```
   303→
   304→```bash
   305→# List connected MCPs
   306→curl http://localhost:8080/api/mcps
   307→
   308→# Reload after adding/editing config files
   309→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/mcps/reload
   310→```
   311→
   312→## Shell Access
   313→
   314→Shell access is **disabled by default**. Toggle it at runtime:
   315→
   316→```bash
   317→# Enable
   318→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/enable
   319→
   320→# Disable
   321→curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access/disable
   322→
   323→# Check status
   324→curl http://localhost:8080/api/shell-access
   325→```
   326→
   327→When enabled, the AI can execute arbitrary commands on the host system.
   328→
   329→## Scheduled Jobs
   330→
   331→The AI can create cron jobs via tool calls. You can also manage them through REST:
   332→
   333→```bash
   334→# List all jobs
   335→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs
   336→
   337→# View execution results
   338→curl http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/results
   339→
   340→# Cancel a job
   341→curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8080/api/jobs/{id}
   342→```

   344→## API Reference
   345→
   346→| Method | Endpoint | Description |
   347→|--------|----------|-------------|
   348→| `GET` | `/api/providers` | List registered providers |
   349→| `POST` | `/api/providers` | Register a new provider |
   350→| `DELETE` | `/api/providers/{id}` | Remove a provider |
   351→| `POST` | `/api/chat/{providerId}` | Stateless chat |
   352→| `POST` | `/api/conversations` | Create a conversation |
   353→| `PATCH` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Set provider for next prompt(s) (e.g. providerId) |
   354→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages` | Send a message |
   355→| `POST` | `/api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream` | Send a message (SSE stream) |
   356→| `GET` | `/api/conversations` | List conversations |
   357→| `DELETE` | `/api/conversations/{id}` | Delete a conversation |
   358→| `GET` | `/api/mcps` | List connected MCP servers |
   359→| `POST` | `/api/mcps/reload` | Reload MCP configurations |
   360→| `GET` | `/api/shell-access` | Shell access status |
   361→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/enable` | Enable shell access |
   362→| `POST` | `/api/shell-access/disable` | Disable shell access |
   363→| `GET` | `/api/jobs` | List scheduled jobs |
   364→| `GET` | `/api/jobs/results` | View job execution results |
   365→| `DELETE` | `/api/jobs/{id}` | Cancel a job |

   367→## Data Directory
   368→
   369→All persistent data lives in `~/.aidaemon/`:
   370→
   371→```
   372→~/.aidaemon/
   373→├── providers.json     # Registered LLM providers and API keys
   374→├── memory.json        # AI's persistent memory
   375→├── jobs.json          # Scheduled job definitions
   376→├── conversations/     # Conversation JSON files (stateful chats)
   377→├── skills/            # Skill folders (SKILL.md + resources)
   378→└── mcps/              # MCP server config JSON files
   379→```

   381→## Configuration
   382→
   383→Optional `application.yaml` / env properties:
   384→
   385→| Property | Default | Description |
   386→|----------|---------|-------------|
   387→| `aidaemon.config-dir` | `~/.aidaemon` | Data directory |
   388→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.enabled` | `false` | Enable adding MCP servers from Smithery via chat |
   389→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.namespace` | — | Smithery namespace (from dashboard) |
   390→| `aidaemon.smithery-mcp.api-key` | — | Smithery API key (from dashboard) |
   391→| `aidaemon.shell-access` | `false` | Allow AI shell execution |
   392→| `aidaemon.delegation-enabled` | `false` | Enable sub-agent delegation |
   393→| `aidaemon.delegation-threshold-seconds` | `30` | Estimated seconds above which the model should delegate |
   394→| `aidaemon.context-window.chars-limit` | `0` | Max characters for conversation history (and, for named bots, conversation + personal memory combined). Use with `retrieveOlderMessages` for long chats |
   395→| `aidaemon.context-window.personal-memory-ratio` | `0` | For named bots only: share of chars-limit used for personal memory (0–1). Remainder is for conversation history |
   396→| `aidaemon.system-instructions` | (see application.yaml) | System prompt for the model |
   397→
   398→## Caution
   399→
   400→- **API keys are stored in plain text** in `~/.aidaemon/providers.json`. Protect this file accordingly.
   401→- **Shell access grants the AI full command execution** on your machine. Only enable it in trusted environments and disable it when not needed.
   402→- **Scheduled jobs run autonomously** using your configured provider and consume API tokens. Monitor your jobs and cancel any you no longer need.
   403→- **MCP servers may expose powerful capabilities** (file system access, API calls, etc.). Review MCP configurations before connecting them.
   404→- This project uses Spring AI milestone releases which may have breaking changes between versions.

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report: AI Daemon

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's the complete understanding of the AI Daemon project:

### 1. **Overall Project Structure**

**Technology Stack:**
- **Backend:** Java 25, Spring Boot 4.0.3, Spring AI 2.0.0-M2
- **Frontend:** React, TypeScript, Vite
- **Build Tool:** Maven with frontend-maven-plugin
- **Database:** File-based JSON storage (no relational DB)

**Key Dependencies:**
- Spring AI (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Gemini)
- Model Context Protocol (MCP) - version 0.18.1
- Jackson for JSON serialization
- Spring WebFlux for reactive streams
- Spring Web for REST APIs

**Project Structure:**
```
aidaemon/
├── src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/
│   ├── bot/              (Bot definitions and services)
│   ├── chat/             (Chat engine, conversations, tools)
│   ├── delegation/       (Sub-agent delegation)
│   ├── files/            (File attachment handling)
│   ├── mcp/              (MCP server integration)
│   ├── provider/         (LLM provider configuration)
│   ├── scheduling/       (Job scheduling)
│   ├── skills/           (Skill management and tools)
│   └── web/              (REST controllers)
├── frontend/webui/       (React TypeScript app)
├── pom.xml               (Maven configuration)
└── src/main/resources/application.yaml
```

---

### 2. **File Attachments for User Messages**

**Key Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/files/FileAttachment.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/files/FileStorageService.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ChatMessage.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/web/FileController.java`

**How It Works:**

1. **FileAttachment Record** (Simple data model):
   ```java
   public record FileAttachment(String id, String name, String mimeType) {}
   ```

2. **ChatMessage Storage**:
   - Each message can contain a list of `FileAttachment` objects
   - Files are stored separately with metadata (`id.meta`) and binary data
   - Storage path: `~/.aidaemon/conversations/{conversationId}/files/`

3. **File Upload Process**:
   - Frontend calls `POST /api/conversations/{conversationId}/files` with multipart form data
   - `FileController.upload()` receives the file
   - `FileStorageService.store()` generates a UUID, saves file binary + metadata JSON
   - Returns `FileAttachment` with id, name, mimetype
   - File is persisted in `~/.aidaemon/conversations/{conversationId}/files/` with structure:
     - `{uuid}.{extension}` - binary file
     - `{uuid}.meta` - JSON metadata

4. **File Retrieval**:
   - `GET /api/files/{id}` downloads the file
   - System searches all conversation directories to find the file ID
   - Returns file with appropriate Content-Type header

5. **Message Interleaving**:
   - When user sends a message with files, the content is JSON-formatted with `user_parts`:
   ```json
   {
     "user_parts": [
       {"type": "text", "content": "what is"},
       {"type": "file", "id": "...", "name": "image.png", "mimeType": "image/png"},
       {"type": "text", "content": "this image"}
     ]
   }
   ```
   - This preserves the text/file interleaving in the conversation

6. **Frontend Upload Flow** (ChatWindow.tsx):
   - User clicks file attachment button
   - `handleFileSelect()` calls `api.uploadFile(conversationId, file)`
   - Uploaded file creates a visual "chip" in the contentEditable input
   - Chip contains: file ID, name, mimeType, and a visual X to remove it
   - When sent, all file IDs are passed to the API

---

### 3. **How AI Tools Are Defined and Used**

**Tool Definition Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ChatToolCallbacksService.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/skills/ChatTools.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/delegation/DelegationTools.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/skills/ShellTool.java`

**Tool Architecture:**

1. **Tool Registration** (`ChatToolCallbacksService.buildToolCallbacks()`):
   - Collects tools from multiple sources:
     - `ChatTools` - memory, skills, scheduling, bot-to-bot messaging
     - `ShellTool` - shell command execution (guarded by toggle)
     - `BotToBotTool` - direct bot-to-bot communication
     - MCP server tools (from connected MCP servers)
     - `SmitheryMcpTool` / `SmitherySkillTool` (external integrations)
     - `DelegationTools` - sub-agent delegation (if enabled)

2. **Core Tools in ChatTools**:
   - `saveMemory(key, value)` - Persist information across sessions
   - `readSkillFile(relativePath)` - Read skill resources
   - `listSkills()` - List installed skills
   - `listSkillFiles(skillName)` - Browse skill contents
   - `removeSkill(skillName)` - Uninstall skills
   - `scheduleJob(cronExpression, instruction, description)` - Create recurring jobs
   - `retrieveOlderMessages(startIndex)` - Fetch trimmed conversation history
   - `retrieveOlderPersonalMemory(startIndex)` - Fetch older memory entries

3. **Tool Execution Flow**:
   - Tools are wrapped in `LoggingToolCallback` for logging and approval
   - Each tool is wrapped by Spring AI's `ToolCallbacks.from(toolObject)`
   - Tools use `@Tool` annotation with `@ToolParam` for parameters
   - Tools are passed to the chat model during prompt creation
   - Results are collected and streamed back as `tool` type chunks

4. **Tool Approval** (when `aidaemon.manual-approve=true`):
   - Tools wait for approval before execution
   - Pending approvals sent to UI as `tool_pending` stream chunks
   - UI displays `ToolApprovalCard` with tool name and input JSON
   - User approves/rejects via endpoints: `/api/tools/{approvalId}/approve|reject`

---

### 4. **How the Web UI Renders Messages and Files**

**Frontend Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/components/ChatWindow.tsx`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/App.tsx`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/api.ts`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/types.ts`

**Message Rendering Pipeline:**

1. **Types** (types.ts):
   ```typescript
   interface ChatMessage {
     role: string;
     content: string;
     timestampMillis?: number;
     files?: FileAttachment[];
   }
   
   interface FileAttachment {
     id: string;
     name: string;
     mimeType: string;
   }
   ```

2. **Display Logic** (ChatWindow.tsx):
   - `getDisplayMessages()` - Converts stored messages to display format
   - `parseStructuredContent()` - Parses JSON response with `{"parts": [...]}`
   - `parseUserParts()` - Parses user messages with interleaved files
   - Messages have two formats:
     - Stored format: `{"user_parts": [{type, content}]}`
     - Response format: `{"parts": [{type: "answer"|"tool"|"reasoning", content}]}`

3. **Message Entry Component**:
   - Renders role label, timestamp, collapse/expand button
   - For user messages: displays text + file chips (with download links)
   - For assistant messages: displays answer, tool calls (collapsible), thinking (collapsible)
   - File chips show icon + filename, clickable to download via `window.open(/api/files/{id})`

4. **File Management in Input**:
   - Input is a contentEditable div with embedded file chips
   - Each chip has `data-fileId`, `data-fileName`, `data-fileMimeType` attributes
   - `extractEditableContent()` reconstructs user parts array from DOM
   - When send button clicked, files are extracted and passed as file IDs to API

5. **Streaming**:
   - Server-Sent Events (SSE) at `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream`
   - Chunks arrive with type: `reasoning` | `answer` | `tool` | `tool_pending`
   - `sendMessageStream()` parses SSE and calls `onChunk(StreamChunk)`
   - UI accumulates chunks into `StreamingContent` state
   - Display updates in real-time as chunks arrive

6. **Tool Approval UI**:
   - `ToolApprovalCard` component displays pending approval
   - Shows tool name and formatted JSON input
   - Approve/reject buttons with separate styling
   - Matching tool chunks trigger removal from pending list

---

### 5. **How OpenAI/Image Generation Clients Are Configured**

**Configuration Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/provider/ChatModelFactory.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/provider/ProviderConfig.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/resources/application.yaml`

**Provider Configuration:**

1. **ProviderConfig Record**:
   ```java
   public record ProviderConfig(
     String id,
     String name,
     ProviderType type,      // OPENAI, ANTHROPIC, OLLAMA, GEMINI
     String apiKey,
     String baseUrl,
     String model)
   ```

2. **ChatModelFactory - Provider Creation**:

   **OpenAI:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createOpenAi(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var apiBuilder = OpenAiApi.builder()
       .apiKey(config.apiKey() != null ? config.apiKey() : "unused");
     if (config.baseUrl() != null) {
       apiBuilder.baseUrl(config.baseUrl());  // Support custom base URL
     }
     var options = OpenAiChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "gpt-4o")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .build();
     return OpenAiChatModel.builder()
       .openAiApi(apiBuilder.build())
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Anthropic:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createAnthropic(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = AnthropicApi.builder().apiKey(config.apiKey()).build();
     return AnthropicChatModel.builder()
       .anthropicApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(buildAnthropicOptions(config, tools))
       .build();
   }
   
   private AnthropicChatOptions buildAnthropicOptions(...) {
     return AnthropicChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "claude-sonnet-4-20250514")
       .maxTokens(4096)
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .cacheOptions(AnthropicCacheOptions.builder()
         .strategy(AnthropicCacheStrategy.CONVERSATION_HISTORY)
         .messageTypeTtl(MessageType.SYSTEM, AnthropicCacheTtl.ONE_HOUR)
         .build())
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Gemini:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createGemini(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = OpenAiApi.builder()
       .apiKey(config.apiKey())
       .baseUrl(config.baseUrl() != null ? config.baseUrl() 
         : "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai")
       .build();
     var options = OpenAiChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "gemini-2.0-flash")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .promptCacheKey("aidaemon:" + config.id())
       .build();
     return OpenAiChatModel.builder()
       .openAiApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Ollama** (Local):
   ```java
   private ChatModel createOllama(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = OllamaApi.builder()
       .baseUrl(config.baseUrl() != null ? config.baseUrl() : "http://localhost:11434")
       .build();
     var options = OllamaChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "llama3.2")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .build();
     return OllamaChatModel.builder()
       .ollamaApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

3. **Image Handling in Messages**:
   - File attachments are converted to Spring AI `Media` objects
   - In `ChatContextBuilder.processFileForCurrentMessage()`:
     - For images (jpeg, png, webp, gif): added to UserMessage as Media
     - For text files: appended as text content with file references
   - Media is loaded from storage via `FileStorageService.getBytes()`
   - Passed to model in `UserMessage.media(mediaList)`

4. **Configuration via application.yaml**:
   - No hardcoded API keys
   - Keys stored in `~/.aidaemon/providers.json` (plain text)
   - Provider toggled per conversation
   - Base URL overridable for custom deployments

---

### 6. **Where Conversation Files Are Stored**

**Storage Location:**
```
~/.aidaemon/
├── conversations/
│   ├── {conversation-id}/
│   │   ├── conversation.json     # Main conversation data
│   │   └── files/
│   │       ├── {file-uuid}.{ext} # Binary file

```

**ConversationRepository** (`/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ConversationRepository.java`):
- Loads conversations on startup from disk
- Keeps in-memory cache (ConcurrentHashMap)

     "user_parts": [
       {"type": "text", "content": "what is"},
       {"type": "file", "id": "...", "name": "image.png", "mimeType": "image/png"},
       {"type": "text", "content": "this image"}
     ]
   }
   ```
   - This preserves the text/file interleaving in the conversation

6. **Frontend Upload Flow** (ChatWindow.tsx):
   - User clicks file attachment button
   - `handleFileSelect()` calls `api.uploadFile(conversationId, file)`
   - Uploaded file creates a visual "chip" in the contentEditable input
   - Chip contains: file ID, name, mimeType, and a visual X to remove it
   - When sent, all file IDs are passed to the API

### 3. **How AI Tools Are Defined and Used**

**Tool Definition Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ChatToolCallbacksService.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/skills/ChatTools.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/delegation/DelegationTools.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/skills/ShellTool.java`

**Tool Architecture:**

1. **Tool Registration** (`ChatToolCallbacksService.buildToolCallbacks()`):
   - Collects tools from multiple sources:
     - `ChatTools` - memory, skills, scheduling, bot-to-bot messaging
     - `ShellTool` - shell command execution (guarded by toggle)
     - `BotToBotTool` - direct bot-to-bot communication
     - MCP server tools (from connected MCP servers)
     - `SmitheryMcpTool` / `SmitherySkillTool` (external integrations)
     - `DelegationTools` - sub-agent delegation (if enabled)

2. **Core Tools in ChatTools**:
   - `saveMemory(key, value)` - Persist information across sessions
   - `readSkillFile(relativePath)` - Read skill resources
   - `listSkills()` - List installed skills
   - `listSkillFiles(skillName)` - Browse skill contents
   - `removeSkill(skillName)` - Uninstall skills
   - `scheduleJob(cronExpression, instruction, description)` - Create recurring jobs
   - `retrieveOlderMessages(startIndex)` - Fetch trimmed conversation history
   - `retrieveOlderPersonalMemory(startIndex)` - Fetch older memory entries

3. **Tool Execution Flow**:
   - Tools are wrapped in `LoggingToolCallback` for logging and approval
   - Each tool is wrapped by Spring AI's `ToolCallbacks.from(toolObject)`
   - Tools use `@Tool` annotation with `@ToolParam` for parameters
   - Tools are passed to the chat model during prompt creation
   - Results are collected and streamed back as `tool` type chunks

4. **Tool Approval** (when `aidaemon.manual-approve=true`):
   - Tools wait for approval before execution
   - Pending approvals sent to UI as `tool_pending` stream chunks
   - UI displays `ToolApprovalCard` with tool name and input JSON
   - User approves/rejects via endpoints: `/api/tools/{approvalId}/approve|reject`

---

### 4. **How the Web UI Renders Messages and Files**

**Frontend Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/components/ChatWindow.tsx`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/App.tsx`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/api.ts`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/frontend/webui/src/types.ts`

**Message Rendering Pipeline:**

1. **Types** (types.ts):
   ```typescript
   interface ChatMessage {
     role: string;
     content: string;
     timestampMillis?: number;
     files?: FileAttachment[];
   }
   
   interface FileAttachment {
     id: string;
     name: string;
     mimeType: string;
   }
   ```

2. **Display Logic** (ChatWindow.tsx):
   - `getDisplayMessages()` - Converts stored messages to display format
   - `parseStructuredContent()` - Parses JSON response with `{"parts": [...]}`
   - `parseUserParts()` - Parses user messages with interleaved files
   - Messages have two formats:
     - Stored format: `{"user_parts": [{type, content}]}`
     - Response format: `{"parts": [{type: "answer"|"tool"|"reasoning", content}]}`

3. **Message Entry Component**:
   - Renders role label, timestamp, collapse/expand button
   - For user messages: displays text + file chips (with download links)
   - For assistant messages: displays answer, tool calls (collapsible), thinking (collapsible)
   - File chips show icon + filename, clickable to download via `window.open(/api/files/{id})`

4. **File Management in Input**:
   - Input is a contentEditable div with embedded file chips
   - Each chip has `data-fileId`, `data-fileName`, `data-fileMimeType` attributes
   - `extractEditableContent()` reconstructs user parts array from DOM
   - When send button clicked, files are extracted and passed as file IDs to API

5. **Streaming**:
   - Server-Sent Events (SSE) at `POST /api/conversations/{id}/messages/stream`
   - Chunks arrive with type: `reasoning` | `answer` | `tool` | `tool_pending`
   - `sendMessageStream()` parses SSE and calls `onChunk(StreamChunk)`
   - UI accumulates chunks into `StreamingContent` state
   - Display updates in real-time as chunks arrive

6. **Tool Approval UI**:
   - `ToolApprovalCard` component displays pending approval
   - Shows tool name and formatted JSON input
   - Approve/reject buttons with separate styling
   - Matching tool chunks trigger removal from pending list

### 5. **How OpenAI/Image Generation Clients Are Configured**

**Configuration Files:**
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/provider/ChatModelFactory.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/provider/ProviderConfig.java`
- `/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/resources/application.yaml`

**Provider Configuration:**

1. **ProviderConfig Record**:
   ```java
   public record ProviderConfig(
     String id,
     String name,
     ProviderType type,      // OPENAI, ANTHROPIC, OLLAMA, GEMINI
     String apiKey,
     String baseUrl,
     String model)
   ```

2. **ChatModelFactory - Provider Creation**:

   **OpenAI:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createOpenAi(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var apiBuilder = OpenAiApi.builder()
       .apiKey(config.apiKey() != null ? config.apiKey() : "unused");
     if (config.baseUrl() != null) {
       apiBuilder.baseUrl(config.baseUrl());  // Support custom base URL
     }
     var options = OpenAiChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "gpt-4o")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .build();
     return OpenAiChatModel.builder()
       .openAiApi(apiBuilder.build())
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Anthropic:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createAnthropic(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = AnthropicApi.builder().apiKey(config.apiKey()).build();
     return AnthropicChatModel.builder()
       .anthropicApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(buildAnthropicOptions(config, tools))
       .build();
   }
   
   private AnthropicChatOptions buildAnthropicOptions(...) {
     return AnthropicChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "claude-sonnet-4-20250514")
       .maxTokens(4096)
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .cacheOptions(AnthropicCacheOptions.builder()
         .strategy(AnthropicCacheStrategy.CONVERSATION_HISTORY)
         .messageTypeTtl(MessageType.SYSTEM, AnthropicCacheTtl.ONE_HOUR)
         .build())
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Gemini:**
   ```java
   private ChatModel createGemini(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = OpenAiApi.builder()
       .apiKey(config.apiKey())
       .baseUrl(config.baseUrl() != null ? config.baseUrl() 
         : "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai")
       .build();
     var options = OpenAiChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "gemini-2.0-flash")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .promptCacheKey("aidaemon:" + config.id())
       .build();
     return OpenAiChatModel.builder()
       .openAiApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

   **Ollama** (Local):
   ```java
   private ChatModel createOllama(ProviderConfig config, List<ToolCallback> tools) {
     var api = OllamaApi.builder()
       .baseUrl(config.baseUrl() != null ? config.baseUrl() : "http://localhost:11434")
       .build();
     var options = OllamaChatOptions.builder()
       .model(config.model() != null ? config.model() : "llama3.2")
       .toolCallbacks(tools)
       .build();
     return OllamaChatModel.builder()
       .ollamaApi(api)
       .defaultOptions(options)
       .build();
   }
   ```

3. **Image Handling in Messages**:
   - File attachments are converted to Spring AI `Media` objects
   - In `ChatContextBuilder.processFileForCurrentMessage()`:
     - For images (jpeg, png, webp, gif): added to UserMessage as Media
     - For text files: appended as text content with file references
   - Media is loaded from storage via `FileStorageService.getBytes()`
   - Passed to model in `UserMessage.media(mediaList)`

4. **Configuration via application.yaml**:
   - No hardcoded API keys
   - Keys stored in `~/.aidaemon/providers.json` (plain text)
   - Provider toggled per conversation
   - Base URL overridable for custom deployments

### 6. **Where Conversation Files Are Stored**

**Storage Location:**
```
~/.aidaemon/
├── conversations/
│   ├── {conversation-id}/
│   │   ├── conversation.json     # Main conversation data
│   │   └── files/
│   │       ├── {file-uuid}.{ext} # Binary file
│   │       ├── {file-uuid}.meta  # File metadata JSON
│   │       └── ... (more files)
│   └── ... (more conversations)
├── providers.json                # LLM provider configs + API keys
├── memory.json                   # Persistent memory across sessions
├── jobs.json                     # Scheduled jobs
├── bots/                         # Bot definitions
├── skills/                       # Skill folders
└── mcps/                         # MCP server configs
```

**ConversationRepository** (`/c/Users/evalvis/Desktop/Projects/AI/aidaemon/src/main/java/com/programmersdiary/aidaemon/chat/ConversationRepository.java`):
- Loads conversations on startup from disk
- Keeps in-memory cache (ConcurrentHashMap)
- Persists on save: writes to `{conversation-id}/conversation.json` in pretty-printed JSON format
- Each conversation is a separate directory
- Deletion recursively removes the entire directory

Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\solid

# Solid Skills: Professional Software Engineering

You are now operating as a senior software engineer. Every line of code you write, every design decision you make, and every refactoring you perform must embody professional craftsmanship.

## When This Skill Applies

**ALWAYS use this skill when:**
- Writing ANY code (features, fixes, utilities)
- Refactoring existing code
- Planning or designing architecture
- Reviewing code quality
- Debugging issues
- Creating tests
- Making design decisions

## Core Philosophy

> "Code is to create products for users & customers. Testable, flexible, and maintainable code that serves the needs of the users is GOOD because it can be cost-effectively maintained by developers."

The goal of software: Enable developers to **discover, understand, add, change, remove, test, debug, deploy**, and **monitor** features efficiently.

## The Non-Negotiable Process

### 1. ALWAYS Start with Tests (TDD)

**Red-Green-Refactor is not optional:**

```
1. RED    - Write a failing test that describes the behavior
2. GREEN  - Write the SIMPLEST code to make it pass
3. REFACTOR - Clean up, remove duplication (Rule of Three)
```

**The Three Laws of TDD:**
1. You cannot write production code unless it makes a failing test pass
2. You cannot write more test code than is sufficient to fail
3. You cannot write more production code than is sufficient to pass

**Design happens during REFACTORING, not during coding.**

See: [references/tdd.md](references/tdd.md)

### 2. Apply SOLID Principles Rigorously

Every class, every module, every function:

| Principle | Question to Ask |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **S**RP - Single Responsibility | "Does this have ONE reason to change?" |
| **O**CP - Open/Closed | "Can I extend without modifying?" |
| **L**SP - Liskov Substitution | "Can subtypes replace base types safely?" |
| **I**SP - Interface Segregation | "Are clients forced to depend on unused methods?" |
| **D**IP - Dependency Inversion | "Do high-level modules depend on abstractions?" |

See: [references/solid-principles.md](references/solid-principles.md)

### 3. Write Clean, Human-Readable Code

**Naming (in order of priority):**
1. **Consistency** - Same concept = same name everywhere
2. **Understandability** - Domain language, not technical jargon
3. **Specificity** - Precise, not vague (avoid `data`, `info`, `manager`)
4. **Brevity** - Short but not cryptic
5. **Searchability** - Unique, greppable names

**Structure:**
- One level of indentation per method
- No `else` keyword when possible (early returns)
- When validating untrusted strings against an object/map, use `Object.hasOwn(...)` (or `Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(...)`) — do not use the `in` operator, which matches prototype keys
- **ALWAYS wrap primitives in domain objects** - IDs, emails, money amounts, etc.
- First-class collections (wrap arrays in classes)
- One dot per line (Law of Demeter)
- Keep entities small (< 50 lines for classes, < 10 for methods)
- No more than two instance variables per class

**Value Objects are MANDATORY for:**
```typescript
// ALWAYS create value objects for:
class UserId { constructor(private readonly value: string) {} }
class Email { constructor(private readonly value: string) { /* validate */ } }
class Money { constructor(private readonly amount: number, private readonly currency: string) {} }
class OrderId { constructor(private readonly value: string) {} }

// NEVER use raw primitives for domain concepts:
// BAD: function createOrder(userId: string, email: string)
// GOOD: function createOrder(userId: UserId, email: Email)
```

See: [references/clean-code.md](references/clean-code.md)

### 4. Design with Responsibility in Mind

**Ask these questions for every class:**
1. "What pattern is this?" (Entity, Service, Repository, Factory, etc.)
2. "Is it doing too much?" (Check object calisthenics)

**Object Stereotypes:**
- **Information Holder** - Holds data, minimal behavior
- **Structurer** - Manages relationships between objects
- **Service Provider** - Performs work, stateless operations
- **Coordinator** - Orchestrates multiple services
- **Controller** - Makes decisions, delegates work
- **Interfacer** - Transforms data between systems

See: [references/object-design.md](references/object-design.md)

### 5. Manage Complexity Ruthlessly

**Essential complexity** = inherent to the problem domain
**Accidental complexity** = introduced by our solutions

**Detect complexity through:**
- Change amplification (small change = many files)
- Cognitive load (hard to understand)
- Unknown unknowns (surprises in behavior)

**Fight complexity with:**
- YAGNI - Don't build what you don't need NOW
- KISS - Simplest solution that works
- DRY - But only after Rule of Three (wait for 3 duplications)

See: [references/complexity.md](references/complexity.md)

### 6. Architect for Change

**Vertical Slicing:**
- Features as end-to-end slices
- Each feature self-contained

**Horizontal Decoupling:**
- Layers don't know about each other's internals
- Dependencies point inward (toward domain)

**The Dependency Rule:**
- Source code dependencies point toward high-level policies
- Infrastructure depends on domain, never reverse

See: [references/architecture.md](references/architecture.md)

## The Four Elements of Simple Design (XP)

In priority order:
1. **Runs all the tests** - Must work correctly
2. **Expresses intent** - Readable, reveals purpose
3. **No duplication** - DRY (but Rule of Three)
4. **Minimal** - Fewest classes, methods possible

## Code Smell Detection

**Stop and refactor when you see:**

| Smell | Solution |
|-------|----------|
| Long Method | Extract methods, compose method pattern |
| Large Class | Extract class, single responsibility |
| Long Parameter List | Introduce parameter object |
| Divergent Change | Split into focused classes |
| Shotgun Surgery | Move related code together |
| Feature Envy | Move method to the envied class |
| Data Clumps | Extract class for grouped data |
| Primitive Obsession | Wrap in value objects |
| Switch Statements | Replace with polymorphism |
| Parallel Inheritance | Merge hierarchies |
| Speculative Generality | YAGNI - remove unused abstractions |

See: [references/code-smells.md](references/code-smells.md)

## Design Patterns Awareness

**Creational:** Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype
**Structural:** Adapter, Bridge, Decorator, Composite, Proxy
**Behavioral:** Strategy, Observer, Template Method, Command

**Warning:** Don't force patterns. Let them emerge from refactoring.

See: [references/design-patterns.md](references/design-patterns.md)

## Testing Strategy

**Test Types (from inner to outer):**
1. **Unit Tests** - Single class/function, fast, isolated
2. **Integration Tests** - Multiple components together
3. **E2E/Acceptance Tests** - Full system, user perspective

**Arrange-Act-Assert Pattern:**
```typescript
// Arrange - Set up test state
const calculator = new Calculator();

// Act - Execute the behavior
const result = calculator.add(2, 3);

// Assert - Verify the outcome
expect(result).toBe(5);
```

**Test Naming:** Use concrete examples, not abstract statements
```typescript
// BAD: 'can add numbers'
// GOOD: 'when adding 2 + 3, returns 5'
```

See: [references/testing.md](references/testing.md)

## Behavioral Principles

- **Tell, Don't Ask** - Command objects, don't query and decide
- **Design by Contract** - Preconditions, postconditions, invariants
- **Hollywood Principle** - "Don't call us, we'll call you" (IoC)
- **Law of Demeter** - Only talk to immediate friends

## Pre-Code Checklist

Before writing ANY code, answer:

1. [ ] Do I understand the requirement? (Write acceptance criteria first)
2. [ ] What test will I write first?
3. [ ] What is the simplest solution?
4. [ ] What patterns might apply? (Don't force them)
5. [ ] Am I solving a real problem or a hypothetical one?

## During-Code Checklist

While coding, continuously ask:

1. [ ] Is this the simplest thing that could work?
2. [ ] Does this class have a single responsibility?
3. [ ] Am I depending on abstractions or concretions?
4. [ ] Can I name this more clearly?
5. [ ] Is there duplication I should extract? (Rule of Three)

## Post-Code Checklist

After the code works:

1. [ ] Do all tests pass?
2. [ ] Is there any dead code to remove?
3. [ ] Can I simplify any complex conditions?
4. [ ] Are names still accurate after changes?
5. [ ] Would a junior understand this in 6 months?

## Red Flags - Stop and Rethink

- Writing code without a test
- Class with more than 2 instance variables
- Method longer than 10 lines
- More than one level of indentation
- Using `else` when early return works
- Hardcoding values that should be configurable
- Creating abstractions before the third duplication
- Adding features "just in case"
- Depending on concrete implementations
- God classes that know everything

## Remember

> "A little bit of duplication is 10x better than the wrong abstraction."

> "Focus on WHAT needs to happen, not HOW it needs to happen."

> "Design principles become second nature through practice. Eventually, you won't think about SOLID - you'll just write SOLID code."

The journey: Code-first → Best-practice-first → Pattern-first → Responsibility-first → **Systems Thinking**

Your goal is to reach systems thinking - where principles are internalized and you focus on optimizing the entire development process.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\evalvis\.claude\skills\git-conversation-history

# Git Conversation History

## Purpose

Every commit can have its full Claude Code conversation saved alongside it in:
```
.claude/git-conversation-history/<commit-sha>.jsonl
```

Before modifying an existing feature, find relevant commit(s), read conversation history (scripts provided below) to understand the original intent, constraints, and decisions — so you don't introduce bugs.
Recommended approach:
1. After understanding user prompt, find relevant commit(s) by the title.
2. Read commit conversation history (use scripts below).
3. You now have better context.
4. When you do a feature either use commit or light commit (scripts below) so you save the conversation context for the future.

---

## OS Detection

Detect the OS at the start of any session and use the correct script folder:

| OS | Folder | Run with |
|----|--------|----------|
| Linux / macOS | `bash/` | `bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/<script>.sh` |
| Windows | `powershell/` | Use `pwsh` if available, otherwise `powershell`. Run: `pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` or `powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/<script>.ps1` |

To detect which is available on Windows, run: `pwsh -Version 2>/dev/null || powershell -Command '$PSVersionTable.PSVersion'`

---

## Scripts

Note: Before executing script examples check if they are in global .claude or local .claude.

---

### 1a. Commit with full conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Runs `git commit`, then copies the **entire** latest Claude Code session to `.claude/git-conversation-history/<sha>.jsonl`. Use for the first commit on a feature, or when you want a full snapshot.

---

### 1b. Commit with light (incremental) conversation saved

Stage files you want to commit before executing this script.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/commit-light.sh "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (pwsh / PowerShell 7+)
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]

# Windows (powershell / Windows PowerShell 5.x fallback)
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/commit-light.ps1 "feat: title" ["optional description"]
```

Same as above, but **only saves new conversation lines since the previous commit's history**. Avoids duplicating earlier history across every commit.

**How it works:** finds the last line of the previous commit's `.jsonl`, locates it verbatim in the current session file, and saves only everything after it.

**Fallback behaviour:**
- No previous history file → saves full session (same as `commit.sh`)
- Previous history anchor not found in session (e.g. session was rotated) → saves full session
- No new lines since last commit → saves an empty file

**Recommended default** for all commits after the first on a branch.

---

### 2. Save conversation for an existing commit (retroactive)

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/save-history.sh <commit-sha>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/save-history.ps1 <commit-sha>
```

---

### 3. Count lines in history file(s)

Check file size before reading deeply.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/count-lines.sh [commit-sha]

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/count-lines.ps1 [commit-sha]
```

Omit commit SHA to list all history files with sizes.

---

### 4. Read a line range from a history file

Conversation files can be very large. Use `count-lines` first to know the total, then read the range you need.

```bash
# Linux/macOS
bash .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/bash/get-history-lines.sh <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>

# Windows
pwsh .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
# or
powershell .claude/skills/git-conversation-history/powershell/get-history-lines.ps1 <commit-sha> <start_line> <end_line>
```

All three arguments are required. Partial SHAs are accepted — e.g. `a3f9c12` will match `a3f9c12abc.jsonl`.

Each output line is prefixed with its line number: `42: {"role": ...}`

**Workflow:**
1. `count-lines` to see file sizes
2. `get-history-lines a3f9c12 1 100` to read the first 100 lines
3. Continue with the next range as needed

---

## Storage

```
.claude/
  git-conversation-history/
    a3f9c12.jsonl
    d82bb41.jsonl
```

`.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, Claude Code's native session format
